title: Soft-regime DRO using adversarially estimated group probabilities
summary: Adversarial weighting is already present, but recent PG-DRO work suggests moving from global reweighting to worst-group robustness with probabilistic group membership.
rationale: Adversarial weighting is already present, but recent PG-DRO work suggests moving from global reweighting to worst-group robustness with probabilistic group membership. This fits the competition because regimes are fuzzy rather than hard: wet vs dry, early vs late, testing vs GP, and unusual races. Instead of one domain weight, optimize more against samples likely to belong to hard-shift groups.
implementation_hint: Train a lightweight classifier to estimate soft membership over regime buckets such as `wet/dry x race_phase x tyre_age_bin x year/race cluster`. Use those probabilities to compute probabilistic worst-group weights or to upweight fold losses for samples with high mass on historically weak groups. Compare against the current adversarial weights under the same folds.
expected_effect: Could reduce overfitting to dominant dry-midrace patterns and improve stability on minority regimes without needing fully separate experts.
risk: Weighting may become too noisy and over-focus on tiny groups; needs clipping, shrinkage toward uniform, and strict forward-gap CV to avoid rewarding synthetic robustness.
